title: Needle-felted dormouse
subtitle: A sleeping British dormouse in warm honey-brown merino
excerpt: Needle felt a compact sleeping dormouse curled into a ball with its distinctive bushy tail wrapped around the body. The finished piece is about 5 cm across and makes a charming wildlife ornament or a companion to an autumn wreath.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting workshop traditions. Dormouse forms in a sleeping pose are a seasonal beginner project in autumn and winter felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry method of sculpting wool using barbed needles. Each stab drives the barbed tip through the fibres, catching and tangling them so they bind together into a firm, stable shape.

content.text: The hazel dormouse has a warm golden-brown coat and a full, bushy tail. This version is posed as if curled up asleep, which suits the dormouse's hibernation behaviour and makes the piece easy to model from a single compact core. Use a warm honey or amber merino for the body and a slightly paler shade for the underside.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Keep a finger guard on the hand holding the work.

items.name: Merino roving, honey-brown or amber (body and tail)
Merino roving, cream or pale beige (underside)

content.text: Take 8 g of brown merino and roll it into a tight ball. Needle all over with the size-38 needle to compact it into a round form about 4 cm across. The sleeping pose means the body is nearly spherical; keep rotating the core to maintain an even round shape.

Lay a patch of pale cream merino over the lower half of the ball and needle it in smoothly. The cream should cover roughly the lower third of the visible surface, fading into the brown at the sides. Use the size-40 needle to blend the colour boundary so it appears as a gradual change rather than a hard line.

Pull a long, slightly fluffy rope of brown merino about 15 cm long. Needle it lightly on the mat to give it a little structure without making it too dense; the tail should remain slightly fluffy. Coil the tail around the body so the tip curls up toward the face and needle the base end into the body to secure it. The tail wraps around about three-quarters of the body circumference.
Step 4: Add the head and face

The sleeping pose does not require a separate head. Push down one section of the upper surface slightly to suggest where the tucked-in head would be. Needle two small ear shapes from thin brown merino and attach them to the top of the body at the head-position. Add two tiny dark eyes just visible above the tail tip. For the
finish, work the size-40 needle lightly over the whole surface to smooth any rough patches.

items.problem: The tail detaches from the body after a few days
items.cause: The tail base was not needled deeply enough into the body
items.fix: Pull the tail back and needle the base again with a dozen firm passes directly into the body surface. Add a small wisp of merino over the join and needle it flat to lock the tail base in place.

items.problem: The body looks angular rather than round
items.cause: Needle pressure was concentrated in a few areas rather than distributed evenly
items.fix: Work in a regular rotation: a few passes on one face, then turn and work the next face, continuing all the way around. Even coverage produces an even round shape.
